examples.input: In electoral politics, every politician must cater to voters to get elected. John is a master politician who desperately wants to win, so he will do whatever it takes to appeal to voters. Often, it is impossible to please voters without making many empty promises. In fact, John was elected. Which statement is correct?

Part-time teaching is a significant source of actual income for university faculty. Statistics from a university indicate that the average weekly hours of part-time teaching for faculty in the Law Department is 3.5, whereas it is 1.8 for the Accounting Department. Consequently, the current average actual income of the Law Department faculty at this university is higher than that of the Accounting Department.

Which of the following, if true, would undermine the argument above?

I. The hourly compensation for part-time teaching is generally higher for Accounting faculty than for Law faculty.
II. 35% of the Accounting faculty work as part-time accountants, while 20% of the Law faculty work as part-time lawyers.
III. 48% of the Accounting faculty engage in part-time teaching, whereas only 20% of the Law faculty do so.

The crucial distinction between civilized man and savages or other animals lies in the restraint of instinctive impulses through foresight. True foresight occurs only when a person performs an action not under the compulsion of instinctive impulses, but because his reason tells him that he will benefit from it at some future time. Tilling the soil is an act of foresight; people work in the spring in order to have food in the winter.

Which of the following statements is an assumption upon which the argument above depends?

A doctor administered lycopene capsules extracted from tomatoes to a group of patients awaiting surgery for prostate tumors. The dosage was 15 mg twice daily. Three weeks later, it was observed that the tumors in this group had significantly shrunk, with some almost completely eliminated. Based on this, the doctor inferred that lycopene is effective in shrinking prostate tumors.

Which of the following, if true, provides the strongest support for the doctor's conclusion?

Although the ratio of physicians to the population is roughly the same in the United States and Canada, the United States has 33% more surgeons per capita than Canada. Clearly, it is for this reason that the average number of surgeries performed per person in the United States is 40% higher than in Canada.

The explanation above relies on which of the following assumptions?

Computer antivirus companies classify viruses that have been captured and processed as "known viruses," while all others are classified as "unknown viruses." To date, protection provided by antivirus software against new viruses has lagged behind the emergence of those viruses because the software cannot know the characteristics of a new virus in advance. Some aim to develop antivirus tools capable of active defense against new viruses, but this is impossible; it is as fanciful as trying to create a specific cure for an unknown disease.

Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?

A good system can prevent bad people from acting with impunity, whereas a flawed system can hinder good people from fully accomplishing good deeds, or even lead to the opposite outcome. In this sense, institutions are fundamental. Therefore, we must not only continue to advance economic structural reform but also accelerate political structural reform.

Which of the following options, if true, most strongly supports the argument above?

Forty-five percent of all blood donated in the United States is Type O. Because Type O blood is compatible with everyone, it is indispensable in emergencies where there is no time to determine a patient's blood type. Type O is the only blood type compatible with all others, allowing it to be transfused into any patient. However, precisely because of this utility, Type O blood is in a chronic state of shortage.

If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

A sociologist conducted a study on two groups of adolescents. The first group spent an average of at least 10 hours per week watching visual media containing violence, whereas the second group watched no more than 2 hours. The results indicated that the proportion of individuals exhibiting rude behavior was significantly higher in the first group than in the second. Consequently, the study concludes that frequent exposure to violent visual media tends to cause rude behavior in adolescents.

Which of the following, if true, casts the most doubt on the conclusion of the study above?

The world's first bicycle was invented in 1817. After its appearance, it enjoyed only a brief period of popularity before vanishing, only to reappear in 1860. Why was this the case? A new technology is accepted only when it is consistent with societal values. Therefore, a change in societal values must have occurred between 1817 and 1860.

The reasoning in the argument above is flawed because the argument____

The brains of identical twins are genetically identical. When one member of a pair of identical twins suffers from schizophrenia, a specific region in the brain of the affected twin is smaller than the corresponding region in the brain of the unaffected twin. When neither of the twins has schizophrenia, no such difference exists between them. Therefore, this finding provides conclusive evidence for the theory that schizophrenia is caused by physical structural damage to the brain.

Which of the following is an assumption required by the argument above?

Because the conclusions of quantum theory are counter-intuitive, some scientists hold dissenting views regarding the theory. Although they have attempted to rigorously demonstrate that the assertions of quantum theory are imprecise (i.e., attempted to rigorously falsify it), it has been found that the errors fall within generally acceptable statistical ranges. These results of quantum theory differ from the results of competing theories, which indicates that accepting quantum theory is reasonable.

Which of the following principles most helps to justify the reasoning above?

In August 2014, over 200 Russian trucks transporting relief supplies entered eastern Ukraine. If military operations by Ukrainian government forces in the east directly or indirectly strike the Russian convoy, it could trigger harsh measures from the Russian side; if Ukrainian government forces temporarily cease military operations in the east to ensure the safety of the Russian convoy, it will give the disadvantaged insurgents a chance to regroup.

If the statements above are true, which of the following must be true?

It is well known that high blood cholesterol levels increase the risk of strokes caused by blood clotting. However, a recent report indicates that low blood cholesterol levels increase the risk of another fatal type of stroke—cerebral hemorrhage—which is caused by the rupture of arteries in the brain. The report suggests that because blood cholesterol plays a vital role in maintaining the resilience of cell membranes, low blood cholesterol weakens arterial walls, thereby rendering them susceptible to rupture. Thus, this conclusion supports the view long argued by Japanese researchers: that the typical American diet is better at preventing cerebral hemorrhage than the traditional Japanese diet.

The argument above is based on which of the following assumptions?

An economist argues that by transferring polluting industries from developed countries to developing countries, developed countries will benefit from an improved natural environment. Although developing countries will suffer from environmental pollution, they will be compensated through job creation and increased tax revenue. Therefore, the circumstances of both parties improve, resulting in an increase in total global welfare.

Which of the following is an assumption of the economist's argument?

Certain states in the United States impose a 7% sales tax on the prices of most products purchased within their jurisdiction. Consequently, if this tax were viewed as an income tax, its effect would be the opposite of that of the federal income tax. That is, the lower the income, the higher the percentage of annual income paid in taxes.

The conclusion above can be properly drawn if which of the following is assumed as a premise?

In New York, an interesting phenomenon occurs: there are specific times every night when the city's water consumption surges suddenly. Observations reveal that these times invariably coincide with long commercial breaks inserted into popular television programs. This surge in water usage is attributed to people going to the restroom simultaneously.

Which of the following is the most reasonable conclusion that can be drawn from the phenomenon described above?

Rumors regarding financial disarray are damaging a bank's reputation. If management does not attempt to refute these rumors, they will spread and ultimately destroy customer confidence. However, if management makes an effort to refute these rumors, the refutation serves to increase suspicion more than it allays it.

If the statements above are true, which of the following must be true based on them? ____

For the writers who first coined the term "feudalism," the existence of feudalism presupposed the existence of an aristocracy. Strictly speaking, however, an aristocracy cannot exist unless titles indicating superior status and the right to inherit those titles are recognized by law. Although feudalism existed long before the 8th century, legally recognized hereditary titles of nobility did not appear until the 12th century, by which time many feudal institutions were already in decline.

If the statements above are true, which of the following assertions do they most strongly support?

Proportionally, more people diagnosed with schizophrenia, a brain disorder, are born during the winter months than at any other time of the year. A recent study suggests that the cause may be malnutrition among some mothers during the coldest months of the year. It is during this period that it is most difficult for people to obtain or afford a variety of fresh foods.

Which of the following, if true, most helps to support the conclusion above? ____

In his book Common Sense, Thomas Paine addressed the question of whether monarchy and hereditary succession are legitimate. To those who believed in the legitimacy of the hereditary system, Paine posed and answered the following question: How did the first king originate? There are only three possibilities: by lot, by election, or by usurpation. If the first king was chosen by lot or election, this established a precedent for subsequent kings, thereby negating the practice of hereditary succession. If the first king obtained the throne through usurpation, no one would be bold enough to attempt to justify hereditary succession.

Which of the following best describes the technique employed in Paine's argument?

Since 1997, Hong Kong has experienced a severe economic recession; this was the same year the "One Country, Two Systems" policy was implemented. Some claim that this policy caused the economic decline.
Which of the following questions is most relevant to refuting this line of reasoning?

Currently, regulations requiring private enterprises to establish separate workspaces for smokers and non-smokers constitute an unreasonable infringement on the private sector. The fact, indicated by research, that non-smokers may be harmed by inhaling second-hand smoke is not the primary issue. On the contrary, the primary issue is the government's infringement upon the right of private enterprises to determine their own policies and regulations.

Which of the following principles, if accepted, allows the conclusion above to be reasonably drawn?

The wild Mouflon sheep of Corsica are direct descendants of domesticated sheep that escaped from captivity on the island 8,000 years ago. Therefore, they provide archaeologists with a representation of what early domesticated sheep looked like before the onset of the selective breeding that produced modern sheep.

The argument above assumes which of the following?

A species of microorganism found in Lake Placid usually reproduces at temperatures above freezing. It is currently winter, and the lake has frozen over. Therefore, if the microorganism under study is indeed present in Lake Placid, it is not currently reproducing.

Assuming the premises in the argument are true, what can be inferred?

Jane: Data from an authoritative survey indicate that in the United States, which possesses the most advanced medical technology and facilities, its infant mortality rate ranks only 17th in the world. This leads me to conclude that advanced medical technology and facilities are significantly more effective in protecting the lives and health of adults than they are in protecting infants.

Mark: I cannot agree with your reasoning. In fact, not everyone has equal access to the advanced medical technology and facilities that a country possesses. High infant mortality is more likely the result of low income than of the medical technology and facilities themselves.

Which of the following most accurately summarizes the method Mark uses to counter Jane's argument? ____

The Arctic region abounds in oil, natural gas, minerals, and fishery resources, holding one-quarter of the world's untapped oil and gas reserves. Global warming is causing Arctic ice to melt at a rate of 9% per decade. A shipping route through the Arctic Ocean along Russia's northern coastline, connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans, could shorten the journey from Asia to Europe by over 10,000 kilometers compared to the route via the Panama Canal. Therefore, the development and utilization of the Arctic will bring immense benefits to humanity.

If the statements below are true, all of them weaken the argument above EXCEPT:

According to a 1980 survey, 10 percent of all United States citizens over the age of 16 are functionally illiterate. Therefore, if the projection that the number of United States citizens over the age of 16 will reach 500 million in the year 2000 is correct, we can expect that 25 million of these citizens will be functionally illiterate.

Which of the following, if true, would most seriously weaken the author's conclusion above? ____

Fire ants from Brazil are currently infesting the southern United States in large numbers. Unlike in Brazil, fire ant queens in the United States share nests, with two queens per colony. The fire ants from these colonies are more aggressive than those from single-queen colonies. By exterminating nearly all other insects within their territory, these aggressive fire ants monopolize food resources. Consequently, the fire ant population has exploded. Given that certain insect predators in Brazil are able to limit the growth of fire ant populations there, importing these predators into the United States to curb the fire ant population would, on balance, be beneficial to the local environment.

Each of the following, if true, facilitates the deduction of the conclusion above, EXCEPT:

Challenge is a significant source of self-knowledge because individuals who monitor their emotional and physical reactions when facing challenges can more effectively gain insight into their weaknesses. Which of the following best adheres to the principle discussed above? ____

Scientists hypothesize that a specific fat, "P-fat," is essential for the development of vision. This hypothesis was proposed after observing that infants fed formula with low P-fat content had poorer vision than breast-fed infants, as breast milk is rich in P-fat. Furthermore, it was discovered that infants born 5 to 6 weeks prematurely have poorer vision than full-term infants. Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports the scientists' hypothesis? ____

Excluding corporate purchases, the average expenditure on a new car has increased by 30 percent over the past five years. During the same period, the proportion of the average household budget allocated to purchasing cars has remained unchanged. Therefore, the average household budget must have also increased by 30 percent over the past five years. The argument above relies on which of the following assumptions?

A fuel-efficient car model sells for $37,000, while a standard model sells for $27,000. Based on current gasoline prices and fuel consumption data for both vehicles, a buyer of the fuel-efficient model would need to drive 300,000 kilometers to recoup the $10,000 price difference compared to the standard model. If gasoline prices rise in the future, the mileage required to recoup this price difference will increase accordingly.

Which of the following statements most accurately identifies the flaw in the argument above? ____

Prior to the commencement of a weight-loss program, patients are tested to determine the average number of calories they burn daily. The program's physicians prescribe a diet for each patient that reduces daily caloric intake by a specific percentage relative to their normal intake. The physicians predict that each patient adhering to this diet will lose weight, reaching a projected target weight. However, the patients fail to lose the predicted amount of weight. Which of the following, if true, best explains why the patients did not lose the weight predicted by the physicians? ____

At the entrance to the changing rooms of a public swimming pool, a notice reads: "Anyone entering the pool area wearing flip-flops will be fined $5 to $10."

A patron asked, "According to relevant regulations, the establishment and enforcement of fines must be carried out by designated authorities. How can you arbitrarily impose fines?"

A staff member replied, "The fine itself is not the objective. The purpose is to use the fine to educate those who lack civic awareness and to maintain the hygiene of the pool."

The logical fallacy committed by the staff member in the dialogue above is most similar to which of the following? ____

Scientist: Just like Earth, Venus possesses a hot molten core that releases immense heat as the planet rotates and orbits. Earth dissipates its internal heat through volcanic eruptions resulting from plate tectonics; however, the absence of volcanic activity caused by similar tectonic processes on Venus is puzzling. Which of the following statements, if true, provides the best explanation for the scientist's puzzle? ____

Due to an aging population, the German government faces a predicament: without reform, the pension system will become unsustainable. Policy options to address this challenge include increasing pension contribution rates, reducing benefit levels, or raising the retirement age. Among these, raising the retirement age faces the greatest resistance; a government implementing this policy risks losing a significant number of votes in the next election. Nevertheless, in 2007, the German government completed the legislative process to raise the retirement age from 65 to 67. Which of the following, if true, best explains why the German government risked adopting this policy? ____

It is truly astonishing that after winning the Nobel Prize in Literature, Márquez could still write Chronicle of a Death Foretold, a genuine tragedy with such a compact and riveting narrative.

The comment above relies on the assumption that: ____

Fossils of a coral species dating back to the last period of glacial formation and polar expansion were discovered on the seabed at depths significantly greater than where the species grows today. Therefore, although the fossils appear little different from the coral growing today, the ability to grow in deep water indicates that they differ significantly in important respects.

The argument above relies on which of the following assumptions?

Lake Dali is a plateau barrier lake formed by volcanic eruptions. The huazi fish—Waki ide—living in this brackish lake, much like salmon in the ocean, must migrate upstream to freshwater rivers to spawn. Although the four rivers currently feeding into Lake Dali are all inland rivers with no outlet to the sea, scientists remain convinced that the Waki ide in Lake Dali originally migrated from the ocean.

Which of the following, if true, provides the best explanation for the scientists' conviction? ____

Tinea is a skin infection caused by a specific fungus. A significant proportion of people with tinea repeatedly exhibit its symptoms. This proves that, for each patient, the tinea was actually never completely cured from the outset.
The argument above assumes that a patient with recurring tinea symptoms: ____

To protect certain nascent industries, the government of Country A has banned the import of products that are currently in the early stages of domestic development. However, the purchasers of these products are several large corporations in Country A that rely on international trade. The import ban on these products has significantly increased production costs for these corporations, severely undermining their competitiveness in the international market.

Which of the following is the most appropriate inference drawn from the argument above? ____

It is widely believed that when a generous patron donates potentially valuable exhibits, the museum effectively receives financial support. However, in reality, donated items require storage space, which is not free, as well as expensive routine maintenance. Consequently, these gifts exacerbate rather than alleviate the museum's demand for financial resources.

Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above? ____

Although inspection systems X and Y operate on different principles, both are capable of detecting and reporting product defects in a timely manner; however, both also erroneously reject 3% of flaw-free products. Since the cost of erroneous rejection is high, money can be saved by installing both systems, rather than just one or the other, and rejecting only those products that both systems identify as defective.

The argument above requires which of the following assumptions? ____

If a company takes highly effective measures to resolve a crisis when it occurs, it can actually enhance its reputation. An excellent reputation can be ruined in an instant by a single event, whereas a bad reputation often requires a long period of effort to eliminate.

If the statements above are true, which of the following assertions is most strongly supported? ____

Proponents of capital punishment typically offer two justifications: first, that the fear of death serves as a deterrent against committing similar heinous crimes; and second, that execution is more cost-effective than its alternative—life imprisonment. However, reliable research indicates that, from an economic perspective, life imprisonment is preferable to capital punishment. The belief that capital punishment saves money is factually inaccurate. Therefore, capital punishment should be abolished.

From a logical standpoint, which of the following is an appropriate evaluation of the argument above? ____

According to statistics from 1999, 10% of United States residents over the age of 35 suffered from obesity. Therefore, if the estimate that the U.S. population will reach 400 million by 2009 is accurate, the number of residents over the age of 35 suffering from obesity will reach 20 million by that year.

Which of the following is most likely the assumption upon which the speculation above relies? ____

Scientific research demonstrates that foods high in unsaturated fatty acids and low in saturated fatty acids are beneficial for preventing heart disease. Fish acquire high levels of the unsaturated fatty acid "Omega-3" by consuming green vegetation in plankton. Similarly, cattle and other ruminants obtain abundant Omega-3 fatty acids by grazing on grass. Therefore, consuming more beef and fish is effective in preventing heart disease. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above? ____

Thick snow cover can sustain unusually severe weather conditions. If a severe winter blizzard blankets the Great Plains, the snow reflects solar radiation back into the atmosphere, thereby maintaining low ground temperatures. Consequently, cold air masses moving south from Canada remain cold enough to trigger additional blizzards. Which of the following conclusions can be properly drawn from the information above? ____
All smart people are nearsighted. I am very nearsighted; therefore, I am smart. Which of the following corresponds to the logical structure of the reasoning above? ____

The Public-Private Partnership (PPP) model enables the government to secure funding while facilitating the entry of private capital into public utility sectors such as electricity and railways. A key risk with this model is that a breach of contract by either the government or the investor can result in economic losses for the other party. In past PPP projects, government defaults have not been uncommon. Despite the frequent occurrence of defaults by local governments, investors remain as enthusiastic as ever about investing in PPP projects. Which of the following, if true, best explains this apparent contradiction? ____

Medical examinations were conducted on students at a secondary school who consumed two to three bottles of beer daily for 60 consecutive days. The results revealed that 75 percent of these students exhibited significant deterioration in liver function. Highly credible experiments have ruled out the possibility that these results were coincidental. If the information above is true, which of the following conclusions is confirmed? ____

It is the people of a country who care most about its destiny and future, and it is they who best understand its situation. Therefore, the path a country chooses to follow must be decided solely by its own people. Which of the following does NOT support the argument above? ____

Velocity Motors launched two compact cars in the spring: the Falcon and the Raven. Both models were well received by car buyers for their novel styling. Both cars came with an owner's manual covering safety features and troubleshooting, as well as a feedback questionnaire to be filled out after one year of use. Fifty-six percent of Falcon buyers purchased car insurance at the time of sale, compared to 82 percent of Raven buyers. One year later, the number of feedback questionnaires reporting problems with the Raven was four times the number of those for the Falcon. Consequently, it is concluded that the quality of the Raven is inferior to that of the Falcon, justifying the fact that a higher proportion of Raven buyers purchased insurance.

Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument above?

All students in a certain class are soccer fans. During the 2014 World Cup, no student who supported Argentina supported Germany, and no student who supported Argentina supported Brazil. However, some students who supported the USA supported Argentina, some who supported the USA supported Germany, and some who supported the USA supported Brazil. If the statements above are true, which of the following regarding the students in this class must be true?

According to a general principle of human nature, the behavior of individuals in society, regardless of their status or level of education, always shifts according to the environment. Human nature contains both good and evil aspects; in reality, everyone possesses an inclination toward self-interest and is referred to as a "rational economic man." Which of the following is NOT implied by the passage above, nor can it be inferred as a conclusion?

Plants must flower in order to produce seeds. There are two varieties of tarragon—Russian tarragon and French tarragon—that appear very similar. Russian tarragon flowers, whereas French tarragon does not; however, the leaves of Russian tarragon lack the distinct aroma that makes French tarragon an ideal seasoning. Which of the following conclusions can be inferred with certainty from the argument above? ____

Human daily thought and action, even the most trivial, involve conscious initiative and a certain degree of creativity, whereas every action of a computer is controlled by pre-written programs. Therefore, computers cannot possess the initiative and creativity inherent to humans. Which of the following, if added, most strongly supports the argument above? ____

A prestigious polling agency conducted a global survey regarding the causes of the September 11 terrorist attacks. The results showed that 40% of respondents attributed the events to unjust U.S. foreign policy, 55% cited the clash between Islamic and Western civilizations, 23% ascribed them to the evil nature of the terrorists, and 19% expressed no opinion.

Which of the following provides the most reasonable explanation for the seemingly contradictory findings described above? ____

Sarah's ideal partner is tall, handsome, and holds a Ph.D. She is acquainted with four men—Arthur, Charles, David, and Brian—one of whom meets all her requirements.

(1) Among the four men, three are tall, two hold Ph.Ds, and one is handsome.
(2) Arthur and Charles both hold Ph.Ds.
(3) Brian and David are of the same height.
(4) David and Arthur are not both tall.

Who meets all of Sarah's requirements? ____

Consider a finite sequence composed of N (N > 1) consecutive natural numbers. Clearly, the subsequences of odd numbers and even numbers are shorter than the sequence of natural numbers itself. However, if this sequence of natural numbers is extended infinitely, the subsequences of odd and even numbers are necessarily not smaller than the whole; in the realm of infinity, a part can be equal to the whole. Which of the following cannot be a logical inference from the conclusion above? ____

People who practice Taekwondo are generally in better physical health than those who do not. Therefore, Taekwondo contributes to improved health. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the conclusion above? ____

Scientists have discovered that the concentration of a protein named "SK3" in the brains of laboratory mice is closely correlated with memory capability across different ages: older mice possess higher levels of SK3 protein, while younger mice have lower levels. Since older mice also exhibit poorer memory than their younger counterparts, scientists conclude that an increase in brain SK3 levels leads to memory deterioration in laboratory mice. Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports the scientists' conclusion? ____

The soccer final of the "2003 Master Cup Robotics Championship" is currently underway, and three professors are predicting the outcome.

Professor Smith said, "The champion is not MIT, nor is it Stanford."
Professor Johnson said, "The champion is not MIT; it is Caltech."
Professor Williams said, "The champion is not Caltech; it is MIT."

The results indicated that only one of them made two correct statements, one made one correct and one incorrect statement, and the third was entirely wrong. Based on this information, the champion is ____

A supermarket frequently encounters customers shoplifting, which negatively impacts the store's profitability. Consequently, the management has resolved to install surveillance equipment and increase the number of floor staff to improve the supermarket's profit margins. Which of the following is most important in evaluating the management's decision?

Prior to the final of the World Table Tennis Championships Men's Team event, the coach of Country S is deciding the lineup. His plan is: If the No. 4 player is in good competitive form and has fully recovered from his injury, then the No. 4 player will play. The No. 6 player will play only if the No. 4 player cannot play.

If the No. 6 player plays in the final, which of the following must be true?

When an osprey returns to its nest from a fishing ground carrying a fish such as a herring, cod, or smelt, other ospreys will retrace its flight path to forage. However, if the osprey is carrying a flounder, other ospreys rarely exhibit this behavior. This occurs even though ospreys consume flounder just as they do other fish.

Which of the following, if true, best explains the phenomenon described above?

The function of a government is to satisfy the genuine needs of the public. However, the government cannot satisfy those needs unless it knows what they are. Freedom of speech ensures that government officials receive information regarding these needs. Therefore, freedom of speech is indispensable for a healthy nation.

Which of the following, if true, does NOT weaken the conclusion above?

A salesperson tells a customer: "The compressor is the core component of a refrigerator. Penguin refrigerators use the same high-quality compressors as Polar Bear refrigerators. Since the price of a Penguin refrigerator is significantly lower than that of a Polar Bear refrigerator, by purchasing a Penguin instead of a Polar Bear, you spend less money while obtaining the same cooling performance."

Which of the following, if verified, would reasonably justify the salesperson's conclusion?

Some people firmly believe that advanced civilizations other than human civilization exist in the universe, simply because there is currently no theory or evidence proving that such civilizations cannot exist.

Which of the following options employs the same line of reasoning as the argument above?

Bracken fern is a toxic weed that has been spreading across the Northern Hemisphere in recent years, destroying numerous pastures. A cost-effective and self-sustaining method to combat this weed is the introduction of its natural predators. Therefore, some scientists propose releasing a moth species native to the Southern Hemisphere, which feeds on bracken fern, into affected regions of the Northern Hemisphere to control the growth of the weed.

If the scientists' proposal to control bracken fern is adopted, which of the following is a necessary condition for its success?

Students should begin studying philosophy at a young age. Otherwise, they will unquestioningly accept certain traditional values, and philosophy teaches them precisely how to question these values.

The argument above presupposes which of the following?

I. Unless students study philosophy at a young age, they will accept any opinion.
II. Even at a young age, students possess the ability to comprehend certain philosophical concepts and theories.
III. It is beneficial for students to be able to question traditional values.

How can airlines prevent commercial aircraft crashes? Studies indicate that two-thirds of such accidents are attributed to pilot error. To address this issue, airlines have upgraded their training programs by increasing classroom instruction time and emphasizing cockpit communication skills. However, it is unrealistic to expect these measures to compensate for a lack of actual flight time. Therefore, airlines should reconsider their approach to reducing commercial aircraft crashes through training.

The conclusion above relies on which of the following assumptions?

After the school copy shop was contracted out, the price for copying increased from $0.35 to $0.40 per standard page, causing dissatisfaction among students. The school board notified the contractor that he must either restore the original copying price or his contract would be terminated. The contractor took measures that allowed him to maintain his overall profit without violating the literal requirements of the board's notice.

Which of the following is most likely the measure taken by the contractor?

In ancient times, a massive earthquake nearly annihilated humanity, leaving only two surviving tribes. Initially, every member of the Umbra Tribe firmly believed that human nature is inherently evil, while every member of the Alba Tribe firmly believed that human nature is inherently good. No individual held both beliefs.

Later, as the tribes grew and propagated, their lineage and belief inheritance followed specific rules:

In a marriage within the same tribe, the child would adopt the parents' belief and belong to their tribe.
In a marriage between different tribes, the child would adopt the mother's belief and belong to the mother's tribe.
It is discovered that a person named Albus believes that human nature is inherently good.

Which of the following statements regarding Albus's identity is impossible?

An increase in the content of high-density lipoproteins in the blood enhances the body's ability to prevent the absorption of excess cholesterol, thereby reducing blood cholesterol levels. Some individuals can significantly increase the levels of high-density lipoproteins in their blood through regular physical exercise and weight loss.

Which of the following is the most appropriate conclusion to be drawn from the statements above?

Three members are to be selected from a group of six engineers—Adam, Bill, Carl, Dave, Eric, and Frank—to form a special task force dedicated to developing the company's next high-tech flagship product. To ensure the effectiveness of the work, the following facts are known:

(1) At least one person must be selected from Adam and Carl.
(2) At least one person must be selected from Bill and Eric.
(3) Neither Carl nor Eric can be selected together with Bill.

Based on the conditions above, if Eric is not selected, which two of the following must be selected?

Bids are being accepted for a shopping mall construction project in a certain city. Four construction companies have submitted bids. For simplicity, we will refer to them as Company A, Company B, Company C, and Company D. Before the winning bid is announced, the managers of each company make a prediction:

Manager A says: "Our company is the most likely to win; the others don't stand a chance."
Manager B says: "The winning company will definitely be either Company B or Company C."
Manager C says: "The winner will be either Company A or our company."
Manager D says: "If one of the four companies must win, it will undoubtedly be us!"

After the results are announced, it is discovered that only one person's prediction came true. Which of the following statements is most likely to be true?

It has been discovered that some employees aged 30-40 in this department have violated work regulations. If the statement above is true, consider the following three assertions:

I. There are no employees aged 30-40 in this department who have not violated work regulations.
II. Some employees aged 30-40 in this department have not violated work regulations.
III. No employees aged 30-40 in this department have violated work regulations.

Which of the assertions above cannot be determined as true or false?

You can fool some people at any time. If the statement above is true, which of the following assertions must be true?

I. Alice and Bob could be fooled by you at any time.
II. You desire to fool people at any time.
III. It is possible for you to fool people at any time.
IV. You can fool people only some of the time.
V. You are fooling people at every moment.

Both overwork and stress inevitably lead to insomnia. All managers at Vertex Corp experience stress. Despite repeated warnings from doctors, the majority of the managers continue to work more than sixty hours per week, while the remainder work exactly forty hours per week. Only employees who work more than forty hours per week are eligible for a specific bonus.

Which of the following conclusions is most strongly supported by the statements above?

Consumer Electronics Monthly publishes an annual ranking of the sales volume of domestic and imported televisions. A management consultant argues that this ranking should not serve as the basis for every consumer's decision regarding which television to purchase.

Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports the management consultant's view?

A public service announcement advises people not to drive after drinking until they feel capable of driving safely. However, in a study conducted at a hospital, subjects questioned immediately after drinking often underestimated the time required to regain their driving ability. This result indicates that it is difficult for individuals who drink before driving to adhere to the advice given in the announcement.

Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports the conclusion above?

A survey of a group of companies indicates that the average age of their general managers is 57. Twenty years ago, the average age of the general managers at these same companies was approximately 49. This suggests that there is an aging trend among general managers in the corporate world.

Which of the following casts the most serious doubt on the argument above?

A global survey indicates that over the past decade: the total number of smokers has remained basically constant; only 10% of smokers change their brand annually (abandoning their original brand for another); and tobacco manufacturers spend 10% of their gross revenue on advertising.

At the year-end board meeting of Company Z, Director A argues that the statistics above indicate that the tobacco industry's returns from advertising exactly equal its expenditures; therefore, such advertising is entirely unnecessary. Director B argues that since the 10% of smokers who switch brands rarely switch to Company Z's brand, the company's advertising expenditure is effectively a financial loss.

Which of the following constitutes the most forceful challenge to Director A's conclusion?

Laws in the United States mandate that both drivers and passengers must wear seatbelts while in a moving vehicle. Some people object to this mandate. They argue that every individual has the right to assume risks they are willing to take, provided that such risks do not cause harm to others. Therefore, the decision of whether or not to wear a seatbelt is a purely private matter, just as it is a private matter for someone to assume the risks associated with stock trading or rock climbing.

Which of the following, if true, most effectively challenges the objection described above?

Four strong volleyball teams—Team A, Team B, Team C, and Team D—participate in a round-robin tournament (where each team plays every other team exactly once). The results are as follows: Team B lost one match; Team C won one fewer match than Team B; and Team B won one fewer match than Team D.

Regarding Team A's ranking, which of the following is true?

Over the past decade, semiconductor production by the United States semiconductor industry has increased by 200%, while production by the Japanese semiconductor industry has increased by 500%. Therefore, Japan now manufactures more semiconductors than the United States.

Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?

According to a survey report released at the "Communications and Health" session of the 1999 annual meeting of the International Health and Care Organization, 68% of brain cancer patients have a history of frequent mobile phone use. This finding strongly suggests that frequent mobile phone use significantly increases the probability of an individual developing brain cancer.

Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the conclusion above?

The World Health Organization conducted a global survey regarding the impact of blood donation on health. The survey subjects were divided into three groups. The first group consisted of individuals with a record of two or more blood donations, with some having donated dozens of times. The second group consisted of individuals with a record of exactly one donation. The third group consisted of individuals who had never donated blood.

The results indicated that the incidence rates of cancer and heart disease were 0.3% and 0.5% respectively for the first group, 0.7% and 0.9% for the second group, and 1.2% and 2.7% for the third group. Based on this, some experts concluded that blood donation helps reduce the risk of cancer and heart disease. These two diseases have become major killers threatening the lives of the middle-aged and elderly in both developed and developing countries. Therefore, blood donation benefits both the donor and the recipient, serving a dual purpose.

Which of the following, if true, would weaken the conclusion above?

I. Individuals over the age of 60 constituted 60% of the first group, 70% of the second group, and 80% of the third group.
II. Donors must undergo strict physical examinations before donating and generally possess a better physical constitution.
III. The number of survey subjects was 1,700 in the first group, 3,000 in the second group, and 7,000 in the third group.

Four cups are placed on a table, each bearing a statement:

Cup 1: "All cups contain fruit candy."
Cup 2: "This cup contains an apple."
Cup 3: "This cup does not contain chocolate."
Cup 4: "Some cups do not contain fruit candy."
If only one of these statements is true, which of the following must be true?

A law firm employs 12 staff members.

①Some staff members can use a computer.
②Some staff members cannot use a computer.
③The director cannot use a computer.

Of the three statements above, only one is true. Which of the following correctly indicates the number of staff members in the firm who can use a computer?

Without a fighting spirit, significant achievement is impossible. Leo possesses a strong fighting spirit; therefore, he is certain to succeed.

Which of the following, if true, would make the argument above valid?

The son is permitted to stay at home when only one of his parents is away. If both parents are away, a nanny must be present in order for the son to remain at home.

Based on the statements above, which of the following conclusions can be drawn?

If you violate the law, you will face legal sanctions; if you face legal sanctions, others will look down on you; if others look down on you, you will not be respected; and only if you are respected by others can you live a content life.

From the statements above, which of the following conclusions can be drawn?

Teacher: "If you do not finish your homework, you cannot go out to play."
Student: "Teacher, I have finished my homework, so I can go out to play!"
Teacher: "Incorrect. I merely said that if you do not finish your homework, you cannot go out to play."

All of the following can be inferred from the dialogue above EXCEPT:

Assume the following statement is true: "If Adam is a manager or Ben is not a manager, then Carl is a manager."

From which of the following premises can the conclusion "Ben is a manager" be derived?

Many individuals claim to be professional soccer players. Although they dedicate a significant portion of their daily lives to training and competing, they do not truly belong to the profession because these activities are not their primary source of income.

Which of the following assumptions most strongly supports the reasoning above?
If Adam attends the banquet, then Ben, Carl, and Dave will all attend the banquet together. If the statement above is true, which of the following must also be true?

If the wind is strong, we will fly a kite. If the sky is not clear, we will not fly a kite. If the weather is warm, we will fly a kite.

Assuming the statements above are true, if we are currently flying a kite, which of the following must also be true?

I. The wind is strong.
II. The sky is clear.
III. The weather is warm.

You and I will go to a karaoke venue together only if she goes. She only visits karaoke venues where dancing is permitted, and all such venues are located in the city center. Her sister will go to karaoke only if you participate.

If the statements above are all true, which of the following must also be true

A mother insists that her son study foreign languages from an early age. The son replies, "I have no intention of becoming a translator when I grow up, so why should I study foreign languages?"

Which of the following is the premise underlying the son's response?

Current electricity rates, for both industrial and residential use, have consistently been low. A certain district has introduced a measure: for electricity consumption exceeding a monthly quota, a higher rate will be applied to the excess usage, regardless of whether the consumption is industrial or residential. This measure is expected to significantly promote electricity conservation within the district.

For the measure described above to achieve its intended objective, which of the following must be true?

I. A significant amount of electricity wastage is caused by the low price of electricity.
II. A significant number of users waste electricity because the price of electricity is low.
III. The magnitude of the price increase for excess consumption is generally sufficient to create financial pressure on users who waste electricity.

In several major nuclear powers, when nuclear testing is effectively restricted, the general public tends to save more money, leading to what is termed "negative abnormal consumption." Conversely, when the frequency of nuclear tests increases, the public tends to spend more money, leading to "positive abnormal consumption." Therefore, when nuclear war becomes a universally perceived realistic threat, the public's desire to limit consumption in order to save decreases significantly, and the likelihood of positive abnormal consumption increases significantly.

The argument above is based on which of the following assumptions?

Experts in the UK studying various forms of stress have discovered that an increasing number of people experience varying degrees of discomfort after using the Internet. According to a sample survey of 10,000 frequent Internet users, one-third admitted to feeling irritable and annoyed after going online, while 44% of users under the age of 20 admitted to feeling tense and irritable. Psychologists believe that a condition known as "Internet Mania" indeed exists.

Based on the information above, which of the following is LEAST likely to be a cause of "Internet Mania"?

Fact 1: Television advertising has become less effective: the proportion of brands promoted on television that viewers are able to recall is gradually declining.
Fact 2: Television viewers recall the first and last commercials in a block of consecutively aired advertisements significantly better than those aired in the middle.

Which of the following, if true, allows Fact 2 to best explain Fact 1?

While many individuals without university degrees have become world-renowned entrepreneurs—such as Bill Gates, a co-founder of Microsoft who did not formally obtain a university diploma—the majority of outstanding management professionals have received a university education, particularly an MBA education. Although obtaining an MBA degree does not guarantee success, it can still be asserted that MBA education serves as a cradle for cultivating modern corporate management talent.

All of the following statements can be reasonably inferred from the passage above EXCEPT:

There are three students: Adam, Ben, and Carl. One was born in Boston, one in Seattle, and one in Washington. Their majors are Finance, Management, and Foreign Languages.

The following facts are known:

Ben does not study Foreign Languages.
Ben was not born in Washington.
Carl was not born in Boston.
The student studying Finance was not born in Seattle.
The student studying Foreign Languages was born in Boston.
Based on the conditions above, it can be deduced that Adam's major is:

It is hypothesized that the yellow color of egg yolks is related to the green plant-based feed consumed by chickens. To verify this hypothesis, which of the following experimental methods is the most reliable?

A college recently conducted a survey regarding the impact of scholarships on learning efficiency. The results indicated that, on average, the learning efficiency of students who received scholarships was 25% higher than that of students who did not. The survey evaluated various metrics, including attendance at self-study sessions, the average time required to complete assignments, and daily reading volume. This strongly suggests that scholarships play a significant role in helping students improve their learning efficiency.

Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above?

Increasingly persuasive statistical data indicates that individuals with certain personality traits are prone to hypertension, while those with other traits are prone to heart disease, and so on. Therefore, with further research into the classification of personality traits, the possibility of preventing and treating diseases by actively modifying behavior and adjusting personality traits will be greatly enhanced.

Which of the following, if true, most effectively refutes the argument above?

The sun rises in the east and sets in the west; society develops, and organisms evolve. These phenomena all reflect objective laws that exist independently of human will. Tom disagrees with this view. He argues that some laws can be transformed. He asserts that since humans can transform everything, they can certainly transform certain objective laws. For instance, does not the law of value obediently serve astute business operators? Have humans not tamed raging floods and turned a disaster into a benefit?

Which of the following most accurately identifies the error in Tom's argument?

A robust legal system or the strong social control capabilities of the governing body are essential conditions for maintaining social stability in a nation. Country Y is socially stable, yet its legal system is not sound. Therefore, the governing body of Country Y possesses strong social control capabilities.

Which of the following arguments is most similar in structure to the one above?

In large amusement parks, live shows are deliberately used to direct the flow of crowds. Performances scheduled during lunchtime are intended to alleviate pressure on the park's restaurants, while evening performances serve a completely different purpose: encouraging visitors to remain in the park for dinner. Although the performances at different times ostensibly serve different purposes, underlying them is a unified objective: ____.

Which of the following options is most appropriate to complete the passage?

A certain virus helps control the population of Gypsy Moths by killing their larvae. Although this virus is persistently present in the larvae, it wipes out the majority of them only every six or seven years, thereby significantly reducing the Gypsy Moth population. Scientists believe that this virus, which typically remains in a latent state, is activated only when the larvae experience physiological stress.

If the scientists' hypothesis described above is correct, which of the following situations is most likely to activate the virus?

All Inuit people wear black clothing, and all indigenous people of North Borneo wear white clothing. No individual wears both white and black clothing. Henry wears white clothing.

Based on the facts above, which of the following judgments must be true?

The battery pack used in Tesla electric vehicles consists of nearly 7,000 Panasonic 18650 cells connected in series and parallel. The safety of Tesla's battery powertrain has faced persistent scrutiny from the automotive industry. A battery expert stated that the probability of a single 18650 cell catching fire in the United States is 0.2 parts per million. Therefore, the probability of a battery pack composed of 7,000 such cells catching fire would be 0.14%. Given Tesla's current sales volume, this suggests that a battery fire accident would occur approximately once every month.

Which of the following, if true, most forcefully weakens the expert's judgment?

Although meteorite craters formed by impacts are found throughout the Earth, they are most concentrated in geologically stable regions. This phenomenon of relative concentration is certainly attributable to the lesser degree of surface change in these geologically stable regions.

Which of the following is an assumption required for the conclusion above to hold?

Exports of United States wood pulp have risen significantly. This increase is attributed to the devaluation of the U.S. dollar, which has made purchasing pulp from the United States cheaper for paper manufacturers in Japan and Western Europe than purchasing from other sources.
Which of the following is an assumption made in drawing the conclusion above?

A recent report established that although only 3% of drivers on Maryland highways have their vehicles equipped with radar detectors, more than 33% of the vehicles ticketed for speeding were equipped with such devices. Clearly, drivers who equip their vehicles with radar detectors are more likely to exceed the speed limit frequently than those who do not.

The conclusion drawn above relies on which of the following assumptions?

The disparity in compensation among senior executives reflects the working style across a company's various departments. If this disparity is large, it incentivizes inter-departmental competition and individual performance; if this disparity is small, it incentivizes inter-departmental cooperation and collective performance. The departments at 3M Company operate in a cooperative manner.

Therefore, which of the following statements is most appropriate as the conclusion of the argument above?

All species of caterpillars produce an identical hormone known as "juvenile hormone," which sustains feeding behavior. Only when a caterpillar has grown to a size suitable for pupation does a special enzyme inhibit the production of this juvenile hormone. This enzyme can be synthesized; once absorbed by immature caterpillars, it kills them by preventing them from feeding.

Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports the view that it is inadvisable to eliminate agricultural pests that undergo a caterpillar stage by spraying the aforementioned enzyme on farmland?

Increasingly persuasive statistical data indicates that individuals with certain personality traits are prone to hypertension, while those with other traits are prone to heart disease, and so on. Therefore, with further research into the classification of personality traits, the possibility of preventing and treating diseases by actively modifying behavior and adjusting personality traits will be greatly enhanced.

Which of the following, if true, most effectively refutes the argument above?

Two companies, Will and Exe, provide 24-hour hotline support for customers using their word processing software. Since customers only call the hotline when they encounter difficulties using the software, and Will receives five times as many hotline calls as Exe, it follows that Will's word processing software must be more difficult to use than Exe's.

Which of the following, if true, most effectively supports the argument above?

During the 1980s, the number of hospital emergency room visits by heroin users increased by more than 25%. Therefore, it is evident that heroin use increased during that decade.

The author's conclusion can be reasonably drawn if which of the following is assumed?
